name: Stored-Program Computer
tagline: General-purpose computation by executing instructions from memory
problem:
Special-purpose machines solve one task at a time. Civilization needs a machine that can be reconfigured by changing instructions rather than rebuilding hardware.

The stored-program concept solves that by placing both data and instructions in addressable memory, allowing complex logic, automation, and later software ecosystems.

The remaining problem for Stored-Program Computer is operational reliability. A reader needs more than a name and a historical note: they need to know what failure this capability prevents, what resources it consumes, what signs show that it is failing, and when it is safer to stop than to improvise. This entry therefore treats stored-program computer as a teachable system with inputs, inspection points, records, and limits rather than as a single trick.
overview:
A stored-program computer has a processor that fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and executes operations on data. The instruction stream is itself data that can be changed, stored, and copied.

In practice, the architecture requires reliable memory, deterministic clocking, and input/output interfaces. For bootstrap scenarios, the key insight is modularity: arithmetic, storage, and control are separable subsystems.

In collapse recovery, stored-program computer must be repairable with local materials, teachable to more than one person, and documented well enough that the method survives injury, migration, or leadership change. In a space-settlement context, the same capability must account for closed-loop resources, contamination control, spare-part scarcity, power budgets, and the cost of mistakes in a sealed habitat. The practical minimum is a controlled trial, written checks, safe storage, and review before scale-up.

principles.name: Boolean logic
principles.explanation: Complex computation is built from simple logical operations and state.

principles.name: Addressable memory
principles.explanation: A mapping from numeric addresses to stored values enables programs and data structures.

principles.name: Fetch-decode-execute cycle
principles.explanation: A repeating control loop turns static circuits into dynamic behavior.

components.name: Input/Output
components.function: Moves data into and out of the machine

components.name: Use-case boundary
components.function: Defines what Stored-Program Computer is meant to accomplish and what it must not be used for
components.madeFrom: Written requirements, hazard notes, local constraints, and responsible operator assignment
components.criticalNote: A vague use case leads to unsafe improvisation.

rawMaterials.name: Electric power
rawMaterials.purpose: Energy for computation, storage, and networking
earthLocations: Electrical grids globally; generators in remote sites
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Solar + batteries is the simplest early-space pathway; plan for tight energy budgets and thermal rejection.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Stable voltage regulation, grounding, and basic power-quality control.

rawMaterials.name: Semiconductor devices
rawMaterials.purpose: Transistors and ICs that perform switching, storage, and signal conditioning
earthLocations: Global semiconductor supply chains; packaged IC distributors
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Early colonies import packaged devices; long-term local manufacture requires clean chemistry and crystal growth capabilities.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: ESD-safe handling; correct packaging, heat sinking, and derating for temperature and radiation.

rawMaterials.name: Documentation and standards
rawMaterials.purpose: Make designs reproducible and debuggable across teams and time
earthLocations: Libraries, universities, standards bodies, archived web
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Mirror core references offline; maintain printed and digital copies with checksums and redundancy.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Versioning, change control, and clear measurement units and assumptions.

rawMaterials.name: Precision manufacturing
rawMaterials.purpose: Reliable interconnects, insulation, and repeatable assemblies
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Prefer modular boards and spares early; long-term: local PCB fabrication and controlled soldering/reflow environments.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Quality control, rework capability, and test instrumentation.

buildSteps.title: Define a minimal instruction set
buildSteps.description: Choose basic operations (load, store, add, branch). Keep it simple enough to implement and test.
buildSteps.warningNote: Complexity kills reproducibility; start with minimal cores.

buildSteps.title: Implement arithmetic and registers
buildSteps.description: Build adders, registers, and buses; validate with test vectors.

buildSteps.title: Add memory and address decoding
buildSteps.description: Provide read/write memory and reliable addressing; verify timing margins.
buildSteps.warningNote: Memory timing violations produce intermittent failures.

buildSteps.title: Add control unit
buildSteps.description: Implement fetch/decode/execute sequencing; validate branching and loops.
buildSteps.warningNote: Control bugs can masquerade as arithmetic faults.

buildSteps.title: Add I/O and bootstrap loader
buildSteps.description: Enable loading programs; add a small monitor program for diagnostics.
buildSteps.warningNote: Without diagnostics, every change is a full rebuild.

history.event: Stored-program ideas formalize and early machines demonstrate feasibility.
General-purpose computers spread into science and industry.
Microprocessors and software ecosystems scale computing.

impact:
Stored-program computers enable software-defined behavior: the same hardware can simulate, control machinery, manage logistics, and communicate knowledge.

In the Codex context, computing is a civilization accelerant but also a dependency cliff: it requires power quality, precise manufacturing, and disciplined documentation.

images.caption: Memory, fetch, decode, execute, write back
